message.content: Adversaries may impair command history logging to conceal their activities on compromised systems. On Linux and macOS, this involves manipulating the HISTFILE and HISTCONTROL environment variables to prevent commands from being logged. Techniques include unsetting HISTFILE, setting HISTFILESIZE to zero, or configuring HISTCONTROL to ignore commands starting with a space or duplicates. On Windows, adversaries can alter the PSReadLine module settings to change the log file path or disable logging entirely. Network devices can also be targeted by disabling historical command logging via CLI commands. The impact of this technique is significant as it hinders forensic analysis and incident response by erasing traces of malicious commands. Common exploitation methods include APT38's use of prepending spaces to commands and SILENTTRINITY's bypassing of ScriptBlock logging. Mitigation strategies involve monitoring and alerting on changes to command history settings, enforcing strict logging policies, and using centralized logging solutions to capture command history independently of local settings.
